race.name: L'Etape Thailand by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Etape Thailand by Tour de France
race.tagline: ASO's Tour de France franchise brings closed-road racing to Southeast Asia -- rotating between Thai provinces with tropical heat, hilly terrain, and the only TdF-branded event in ASEAN.

vitals.location: Rotating: Phang Nga (2018-2022), Phitsanulok (2022, 2025), Udon Thani (2024), Thailand
vitals.distance: 140-166 km (87-103 mi) long course; 65-75 km (40-47 mi) short course
vitals.registration_opens: Typically 3-6 months before event; check letapethailand.com or letapebytourdefrance.com
vitals.entry_fee: ~3,000-5,000 THB (~$85-140 USD) depending on registration tier
vitals.event_date: Varies by year and venue. 2025: Phitsanulok. 2026: TBD
distance_options: The Race (Long): 140-166 km depending on venue, 1,000-1,552 m elevation gain
The Ride (Short): 65-75 km depending on venue, lighter climbing
terrain_types: Provincial mountain roads (Phang Nga, Phitsanulok venues)
vitals.start_time: Long course: approximately 5:30-6:00 AM; Short course: approximately 7:00 AM
vitals.registration: Online via letapethailand.com or thaimtb.com. Super Early Bird, Early Bird, and Regular pricing tiers. Race pack with official TdF jersey included.
vitals.prize_purse: Classification jerseys: Yellow (GC), Polka-Dot (KOM), Green (Sprint). No cash prizes.
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple feed zones with water, electrolytes, fruit, energy bars. Mechanical support on course.
vitals.cutoff_time: Broom wagon enforced. Riders behind time cutoffs are pulled from course.

climate.primary: Tropical -- hot, humid conditions typical of Southeast Asia
climate.description: Thailand's tropical climate is a defining challenge regardless of which province hosts the event. When staged in October-December (Phang Nga, Udon Thani), the northeast monsoon season is transitioning -- expect temperatures of 28-34C (82-93F) with humidity of 70-85% and a risk of afternoon storms. The Phitsanulok May edition falls in Thailand's hot season transition to monsoon, with temperatures reaching 32-38C (90-100F) and building humidity. Early morning starts at 5:30-6:00 AM are essential to complete the long course before peak afternoon heat. Heat management is a genuine performance factor -- riders from temperate climates will lose 5-15% performance without acclimatization.
challenges: Tropical heat: 28-38C (82-100F) depending on venue and month
High humidity: 70-85% throughout the race, limiting evaporative cooling
Monsoon risk: afternoon thunderstorms possible, especially October-November
Early morning starts essential to avoid peak midday heat
Heat acclimatization required for riders from temperate climates -- minimum 7-10 days recommended

terrain.primary: Paved Thai provincial roads through countryside and hilly terrain
terrain.surface: 100% sealed roads through Thai countryside and mountains. Road quality varies by venue -- Phang Nga features winding mountain roads through hilly terrain (80%+ of the province is hills), while Udon Thani is flatter with rolling rice paddies. Phitsanulok offers a mix of countryside and mountain roads near the Phetchabun range. All courses are fully closed to traffic on race day. Surface quality is generally good on main highways, with some rougher patches on secondary provincial roads.
features: Fully closed roads -- no traffic, Tour de France race conditions
Rotating venues showcase different Thai landscapes each year
Phang Nga: coastal mountains, Andaman Sea scenery, Tsunami Memorial start
Phitsanulok: Phetchabun mountain foothills, historical temples, rural Thailand
Udon Thani: rolling Isan plateau, rice fields, traditional villages
Tour de France classification jerseys (Yellow, Polka-Dot, Green)
Two distance options: long course (140-166 km) and short course (65-75 km)
Wave starts for managed field deployment

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: ASO's Tour de France franchise in Southeast Asia, running since 2018 with Move Asia as local promoter. The only L'Etape event in ASEAN, rotating between Thai provinces to showcase different regions. Alberto Contador was inaugural ambassador. Growing but still developing -- the rotating venue model creates inconsistency and limits the brand-building that fixed-location editions achieve.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: experience=4 for Tour de France atmosphere in an exotic Thai setting with jerseys, event village, and cultural tourism dimension. climate=3 for tropical heat and humidity as a genuine performance factor. adventure=3 for the novelty of racing in Southeast Asian landscapes. logistics=3 for reasonable on-course support but significant international travel. prestige=2 and field_depth=2 as a young franchise with primarily recreational domestic field. technicality=1 for 100% paved roads. altitude=1 as courses are low elevation. (36 base + 2 cultural) / 70 = 54.

logistics.explanation: On-course logistics follow ASO standards: fully closed roads, feed zones, mechanical support, chip timing, medical teams, and broom wagon enforcement. The event village runs across the weekend with expo, registration, and post-race celebration. However, the rotating venue model creates logistical uncertainty -- riders cannot plan repeat trips to the same location. International visitors face long-haul flights to Bangkok (BKK), then domestic flights or 5-8 hour drives to provincial venues. Phang Nga is 1.5 hours from Phuket airport, making it the most accessible. Udon Thani and Phitsanulok require domestic connections from Bangkok. Accommodation in Thai provincial cities ranges from basic to comfortable but is not at the level of major cycling destinations.

length.explanation: The long course ranges from 140-166 km depending on venue -- a legitimate full-day challenge, especially in tropical heat. The Phitsanulok edition at 166 km is the longest iteration to date. However, compared to the French original (170 km with 5,400m of climbing) or the most demanding gran fondos globally, these are moderate distances. The short course at 65-75 km is accessible for intermediate riders. The heat multiplier effectively extends the physiological demand beyond what the raw distance suggests -- riding 150 km at 33C feels like 200 km in temperate conditions.

technicality.explanation: Road cycling on sealed Thai highways and provincial roads. Zero technical terrain. The challenge is entirely cardiovascular and thermoregulatory -- managing effort in tropical heat on pavement. Some provincial roads have rougher surface patches, but nothing that requires special equipment or bike handling skills beyond basic road cycling competence. Bring a road bike. Tire choice is irrelevant beyond standard road tires.

elevation.explanation: Elevation varies significantly by venue. Phitsanulok offers the most climbing at 1,552m with 2 KOMs, which is moderate for a 166 km course. Phang Nga's hilly terrain delivers around 1,000-1,200m of climbing. Udon Thani, on the Isan plateau, is the flattest venue with rolling terrain but minimal significant climbing. None of these courses approach the sustained mountain climbing of European sportives or the Phang Nga course is the hilliest, but even that is modest by Alpine or Pyrenean standards. The climbing exists but is not the defining challenge -- the heat is.

climate.explanation: This is the event's defining challenge dimension. Thailand's tropical climate means racing in 28-38C heat with 70-85% humidity regardless of the month. The May Phitsanulok edition is the worst for heat -- approaching 38C with high humidity as the hot season gives way to monsoon. The October-December editions are marginally cooler at 28-34C but with monsoon storm risk. Riders from temperate climates who have not heat-acclimatized will suffer significantly. Expect to consume 2-3x normal water intake and lose 5-15% of normal power output. The 5:30 AM start exists specifically because afternoon conditions become dangerous.

altitude.explanation: All Thai venues are at low elevation. Phitsanulok city sits at approximately 44 meters above sea level. Even the hilliest sections near the Phetchabun range rarely exceed 500m. Phang Nga's coastal mountains top out around 400-500m. Udon Thani is on the Isan plateau at ~180m. Altitude is a complete non-factor. No acclimatization required. No oxygen density considerations. This is sea-level racing in every meaningful sense.

adventure.explanation: Racing in Thailand adds genuine exotic novelty for international participants. Each venue offers distinct Thai cultural landscapes: Phang Nga's Andaman Sea coastline and limestone karsts, Phitsanulok's ancient temple city and rural countryside, Udon Thani's Isan plateau with rice paddies and traditional villages. Starting at the Tsunami Memorial in Phang Nga adds historical resonance. The rotating venue model means no two editions feel identical. But this is a fully supported event on paved roads -- the adventure comes from the Southeast Asian setting, not from wilderness or self-reliance.

prestige.explanation: The Tour de France brand carries global weight, and this is the only official L'Etape event in ASEAN -- a genuine first-mover in Southeast Asian cycling events. Alberto Contador's involvement as inaugural ambassador was a strong credential. However, this is a young franchise (2018) in a region where cycling culture is still developing. The rotating venue model prevents the kind of single-location prestige accumulation that events like Maratona dles Dolomites or L'Etape du Tour itself build. Within Thai cycling, it is the most prestigious event. Globally, it is one of 26+ L'Etape franchise editions without a distinctive identity yet.

race_quality.explanation: ASO standards are upheld through the Move Asia partnership: fully closed roads, chip timing, classification jerseys, feed zones, mechanical support, medical teams, and broom wagon enforcement. The 2018 inaugural with Alberto Contador set a high bar. However, the event has had inconsistency -- COVID cancelled 2020-2021 editions, and the rotating venue model means the organizing team rebuilds logistics in a new province each year rather than refining a single course. Field sizes of 1,000-2,000 are smaller than established L'Etape editions in Brazil (3,500) or Australia. The event is competently run but has not yet achieved the polished consistency of the longer-running franchise editions.

experience.explanation: This is where L'Etape Thailand genuinely delivers. The combination of Tour de France atmosphere -- classification jerseys, professional branding, event village -- with Thailand's tourism infrastructure creates a distinctive cycling weekend. Phang Nga offers post-race beach recovery on the Andaman coast. Phitsanulok gives access to ancient temples and Thai mountain culture. Udon Thani showcases authentic Isan cuisine and rural Thai life. The warm Thai hospitality, affordable street food, temple visits, and the novelty of chasing a yellow jersey through Southeast Asian landscapes create memories that purely European sportives cannot replicate. The post-race tourism dimension elevates the total experience significantly.

community.explanation: Thai cycling culture is young but growing rapidly. The Bangkok and Chiang Mai cycling communities have embraced L'Etape as a prestige event. The 2022 Phang Nga edition drew riders from 30+ countries, though the field is predominantly domestic Thai riders plus expats from Singapore, Malaysia, and Hong Kong. The rotating venue model disperses community building -- regular participants experience different locations but do not build the annual pilgrimage bonds of fixed-venue events. Social media engagement (Instagram @letapethailand) is active but modest. The ASEAN cycling community is building around events like this, but it is not yet the mature community you find around established European sportives.

field_depth.explanation: The field is overwhelmingly recreational -- Thai club riders, expat cyclists, and Southeast Asian cycling tourists pursuing a Tour de France experience. Thailand's competitive road cycling scene is developing but thin. The front of the race features strong Thai domestic riders, but there is no significant international elite presence. The KOM and sprint classifications create competitive structure, but the depth does not compare to established European sportives or the French original. The 2018 edition with Contador was the only time a genuine international name participated.

value.explanation: At approximately 3,000-5,000 THB ($85-140 USD), the entry fee is competitive by L'Etape franchise standards and well below European equivalents. The race pack includes an official TdF jersey, closed-road racing, feed zones, mechanical support, timing, and classification jerseys. For what you get -- ASO-branded event with Tour de France production values in Southeast Asia -- the registration fee represents reasonable value. Super Early Bird pricing offers genuine savings. Thailand's low cost of living means the total trip cost is significantly lower than any European L'Etape edition.

expenses.explanation: Thailand is famously affordable. Accommodation near race venues runs 1,000-3,000 THB ($28-85 USD) per night for comfortable hotels, with luxury options available for 5,000-10,000 THB. Food is extraordinarily cheap -- excellent Thai cuisine at 50-200 THB ($1.50-6 USD) per meal. For international visitors, the main expense is flights -- Europe/Americas to Bangkok runs $600-1,200 USD, plus domestic flights to provincial venues at $30-80 USD. Total trip cost for international visitors: $1,000-2,000 USD including flights, accommodation, food, and entry. For ASEAN-based riders, total costs are under $500 USD. This is among the most affordable L'Etape franchise events globally.

biased_opinion.verdict: Tour de France in the Tropics -- Where Heat Is the Real Col
biased_opinion.summary: L'Etape Thailand brings ASO's Tour de France franchise to Southeast Asia with the ambition of establishing Thailand as a cycling tourism destination. Since 2018, the event has rotated between Thai provinces -- Phang Nga's Andaman coast, Phitsanulok's mountain foothills, and Udon Thani's Isan plateau -- delivering closed-road racing with classification jerseys, TdF branding, and the novelty of chasing yellow through rice paddies, temples, and tropical landscapes. Alberto Contador was ambassador at the 2018 inaugural. The field of 1,000-2,000 riders is predominantly domestic Thai cyclists and ASEAN expats. The defining challenge is not the terrain or the distance -- it is the heat. Racing at 28-38C with 70-85% humidity transforms a moderate 150 km road ride into a survival exercise in thermoregulation. The event delivers a genuinely unique experience -- Tour de France atmosphere at Thai prices in Southeast Asian landscapes -- but the rotating venue model prevents the consistency and identity-building that the best L'Etape franchise editions have achieved.
strengths: Only L'Etape franchise event in ASEAN -- first-mover in Southeast Asian cycling events
Fully closed roads with ASO-standard Tour de France production values
Thailand's tourism infrastructure and affordability make this the cheapest L'Etape globally
Rotating venues showcase diverse Thai landscapes: coastal mountains, temple cities, Isan plateau
Post-race tourism dimension -- beach recovery, temple visits, Thai cuisine -- elevates the total trip
Heat is a genuine and unique challenge that adds a performance dimension absent from European sportives
Growing ASEAN cycling community with international participation from 30+ countries
Alberto Contador as inaugural ambassador set a strong aspirational tone
weaknesses: Young event (2018) -- still building consistency and reputation
Rotating venue model prevents single-location prestige accumulation and course identity
Shallow competitive field -- overwhelmingly recreational participation
Moderate climbing compared to European L'Etape editions -- the terrain alone does not justify the TdF brand
Provincial Thai venues require domestic connections from Bangkok -- logistically layered for internationals
Limited international visibility compared to established L'Etape editions in France, Brazil, or Australia
Field sizes (1,000-2,000) are smaller than flagship franchise events
biased_opinion.bottom_line: L'Etape Thailand is a legitimately well-organized cycling event that leverages the Tour de France brand to create something unique in Southeast Asia. If you want Tour de France atmosphere at a fraction of European pricing, with the added adventure of racing through Thai provinces and recovering at beachside restaurants, this delivers. The heat is the real mountain here -- racing at 33C with 80% humidity is a challenge that Hors Categorie climbers from temperate climates underestimate. The rotating venue model keeps the event fresh but prevents it from building the deep identity of fixed-location sportives. For Thailand-based and ASEAN cyclists, this is the prestige event on the calendar. For global cycling tourists, it is a distinctive destination event worth combining with a Thai holiday, but it is not yet in the same league as the flagship L'Etape editions in France, Brazil, or Australia.

final_verdict.one_liner: ASO's Tour de France franchise in Southeast Asia: closed-road racing through Thai provinces with tropical heat, TdF jerseys, and 1,000-2,000 riders chasing yellow at the most affordable L'Etape edition globally.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want Tour de France atmosphere combined with a Thai holiday at a fraction of European pricing. Yes if you are based in ASEAN and want the most prestigious mass-participation cycling event in the region. Yes if you want to test yourself in genuine tropical heat -- 33C and 80% humidity is a performance challenge that European sportives cannot simulate. Skip if you need deep international competition or prestigious palmares credential. Skip if you want consistent course knowledge -- the rotating venue model means the course changes every year. Skip if significant climbing is your priority -- these courses are moderate by European standards.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the definitive L'Etape experience: L'Etape du Tour de France (France, July). For L'Etape in Asia-Pacific: L'Etape Australia (NSW, November). For the most established L'Etape franchise outside France: L'Etape Brasil (Sao Paulo, September). For a challenging Asian road cycling event: Taiwan KOM Challenge (Hualien, October).

logistics.airport: Bangkok Suvarnabhumi (BKK) -- international hub. Domestic connections required to all L'Etape Thailand venues: Phuket (HKT) for Phang Nga (1.5 hr drive), Phitsanulok (PHS) direct, Udon Thani (UTH) direct.
logistics.alternate_airports: Phuket (HKT) -- international flights available, closest to Phang Nga venue. Don Mueang (DMK) -- budget carriers to Bangkok. Chiang Mai (CNX) if combining with northern Thailand cycling.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Accommodation depends on venue. Phang Nga: Khao Lak beach resorts, 1,500-5,000 THB/night, directly adjacent to race start. Phitsanulok: city hotels, 800-2,500 THB/night, walkable to event areas. Udon Thani: city hotels, 700-2,000 THB/night. Book 1-2 months ahead for race weekend. Thai provincial accommodation is comfortable and affordable but not luxury cycling-destination caliber.
logistics.food: Thailand's street food and restaurant scene is extraordinary and affordable. Race weekend dining at 50-200 THB ($1.50-6 USD) per meal. Excellent carb-loading options: pad thai, rice dishes, noodle soups. Feed zones on course stocked with water, electrolytes, fruit, and energy bars. Post-race Thai food is a highlight of the total experience.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village at the race venue, Saturday before race day. Registration, kit collection including official TdF race jersey, race number, and timing chip.
logistics.parking: Designated event parking at race venues. Provincial Thai cities generally have adequate parking. Shuttles arranged from major hotels.
logistics.travel_summary: Fly into Bangkok (BKK or DMK), then take a domestic flight to the host province -- Phuket (HKT) for Phang Nga, Phitsanulok (PHS), or Udon Thani (UTH). Domestic Thai flights are frequent and inexpensive ($30-80 USD). Alternatively, rent a car in Bangkok and drive 4-8 hours depending on venue. Combine with Thai tourism -- the race weekend is an excellent anchor for a broader Thailand trip.

history.founder: Move Asia (local promoter) under license from Amaury Sport Organisation (ASO -- Tour de France organizer), with support from Thailand Convention and Exhibition Bureau (TCEB) and Tourism Authority of Thailand (TAT)
history.origin_story: L'Etape Thailand launched in October 2018 in Phang Nga province as the first L'Etape by Tour de France event in ASEAN. ASO, Move Asia, TCEB, and the Tourism and Sport Ministry signed a three-year deal to stage the event. The inaugural edition featured a 140 km race and 70 km ride starting from the Tsunami Memorial at Khao Lak, finishing in Takua Pa District. Two-time Tour de France champion Alberto Contador served as event ambassador, lending star power to the launch. The event was positioned as a sports tourism showcase for Thailand's southern provinces. The 2019 second edition returned to Phang Nga successfully, but COVID-19 forced cancellation of 2020 and 2021 editions. The event returned in 2022 with a Phang Nga edition (May) and added a Phitsanulok edition (December). In 2024, it moved to Udon Thani in Isan, then returned to Phitsanulok for 2025. The rotating venue model is deliberate -- promoting cycling tourism across Thailand's diverse provinces.
notable_moments: 2018: Inaugural edition in Phang Nga. Alberto Contador as event ambassador. First L'Etape in ASEAN. 140 km/70 km courses from Khao Lak Tsunami Memorial.
2019: Second edition returns to Phang Nga. Over 300 overseas cyclists participate. Event hailed as sports tourism success.
2020-2021: COVID-19 cancellations disrupt momentum after strong first two years.
2022 (May): Return with 'The Final Great Memory' Phang Nga edition. Riders from 30+ countries, ~2,000 participants. 145 km/75 km courses.
2022 (December): First Phitsanulok edition. 166 km/66 km courses with 1,552m elevation. ~1,000 cyclists on closed mountain roads.
2024 (November): Udon Thani edition. 147 km/65 km courses through Isan rice paddies. Start/finish at Wat Pa Ban Tad.
2025 (May): Return to Phitsanulok. 166 km/66 km courses. Event continues to establish rotating Thai venue model.
history.reputation: The most prestigious mass-participation road cycling event in Thailand and the only Tour de France-branded event in ASEAN. The event has strong institutional support from the Thai government and tourism authorities, positioning it as a sports tourism flagship. Within the broader L'Etape franchise (26+ events in 21 countries), the Thailand edition is a mid-tier franchise -- competently organized but younger and smaller than established editions in France, Brazil, and Australia. The rotating venue model is unique among L'Etape franchise events and keeps the Thai cycling community engaged, but prevents the course-specific prestige building of fixed-location editions.

course_description.character: The character changes with each venue, but the common thread is Thai provincial roads under tropical conditions. The Phitsanulok 166 km course is the most demanding, with 1,552m of climbing through the foothills of the Phetchabun Mountains on winding rural roads. Phang Nga's 140-145 km course traverses the coastal hills of southern Thailand -- 80% of the province is hilly terrain. Udon Thani offers the flattest profile at 147 km through the Isan plateau's rolling rice fields. All courses start early to avoid peak heat and feature fully closed roads with Tour de France classification points.

suffering_zones.label: Pre-Dawn Launch
suffering_zones.desc: 5:30-6:00 AM start in already-warm conditions -- 24-28C at dawn with humidity already building. The field rolls out through the host city before hitting the open road. The first 10-15 km sorts groups as the faster riders push ahead. The key tactical decision begins immediately: pace conservation in heat or competitive positioning.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Urban departure, transition to provincial roads

suffering_zones.label: Heat Builds
suffering_zones.desc: By 8:00-9:00 AM, temperatures climb past 30C and humidity locks in. This is where unprepared riders begin to suffer. The terrain features the course's main climbing sections (Phitsanulok KOMs, Phang Nga coastal hills) or the endless rolling of Udon Thani's plateau. Feed zone discipline becomes critical -- missing a hydration opportunity here means paying for it in the final 40 km.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Main climbing sections or sustained rolling terrain, depending on venue

suffering_zones.label: Tropical Grind
suffering_zones.desc: Past the midpoint, the accumulated heat stress begins to fracture the field. Core body temperatures rise, power output drops, and the mental challenge of continuing to push in 33-35C heat with high humidity becomes the race within the race. The broom wagon is collecting riders who have not managed hydration and cooling. Those who have acclimatized and paced correctly begin to move up.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling to hilly terrain, depending on venue. Heat is the primary challenge.

suffering_zones.label: Final Push
suffering_zones.desc: The last 20-30 km back to the finish under peak midday conditions. Temperatures may reach 35-38C for later finishers. The sprint jersey competitions and final KOM points are decided. The finish line and event village come into view, and the Tour de France atmosphere -- crowds, music, branding -- provides a final motivational push through the heat haze.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Return to host city, rolling terrain, finish line festival

course_description.signature_challenge: The heat. Thailand's tropical conditions transform what would be a moderate 150 km road ride in temperate Europe into a genuine survival challenge. Racing at 28-38C with 70-85% humidity means your body is fighting two battles simultaneously: the physical effort of cycling and the thermoregulatory demand of preventing hyperthermia. Riders who focus solely on terrain preparation and ignore heat acclimatization will fail. The heat is the col that cannot be trained on a turbo trainer.

guide_variables.race_name: L'Etape Thailand by Tour de France
guide_variables.race_location: Rotating Thai provinces: Phang Nga, Phitsanulok, Udon Thani
guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved roads -- fully closed course through Thai countryside and mountains
guide_variables.race_weather: Tropical: 28-38C (82-100F), 70-85% humidity. Early morning start essential.
race_challenges: Tropical heat and humidity -- 28-38C with 70-85% humidity for the duration
Heat acclimatization essential for riders from temperate climates
Early morning start (5:30 AM) to avoid peak midday heat
Moderate climbing (1,000-1,552m) compounded by heat stress

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat acclimatization protocol -- minimum 7-10 days of heat exposure training
non_negotiables.why: Thailand's tropical conditions (28-38C, 70-85% humidity) will cost unprepared riders 5-15% of power output and dramatically increase heart rate at any given effort level. Begin heat training with indoor sessions in warm rooms, overdressed rides, or sauna protocols. Your body needs plasma volume expansion and improved sweat response before race day. This is not optional -- it is the single biggest performance determinant.

non_negotiables.requirement: Hydration and electrolyte strategy tested over 4-6 hours in heat
non_negotiables.why: You will lose 1-2 liters of sweat per hour in Thai conditions. A 150 km race means consuming 6-10 liters of fluid with electrolyte replacement. Practice your hydration strategy in long hot rides -- know your sweat rate, sodium needs, and stomach tolerance for high fluid intake at race effort. Bonking from dehydration at mile 60 in 35C heat is a medical emergency, not just a bad race.

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained aerobic endurance at reduced power in heat
non_negotiables.why: Your target race power will be 10-20% lower than what you would hold in 18C European conditions. Train at this reduced wattage for the full race duration. The mental discipline to hold back in the first 50 km -- when you feel strong in the cooler morning -- is what separates finishers from DNFs. If you try to ride at your temperate-climate FTP, you will implode by mile 50.
